topic: A student finds out their scholarship was meant for someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, official letter felt like sandpaper under my fingertips. I traced the embossed seal, the ink mocking me with its formality. It detailed the full academic award, the prestigious university acceptance, the entire financial lifeline I had worked so tirelessly to secure. Then, the fine print: the intended recipient’s name, a name that wasn't mine. A cold, hollow sensation settled in my chest, heavy and suffocating. I remember staring at the words, the vibrant colors of the acceptance letter draining away until all I saw was a stark, empty room. I crumpled the paper slowly, the sound loud in the quiet dorm room. My hands shook, a tremor running up my arms, and I couldn't bring myself to look at my reflection in the darkened window. Every memory of late-night study sessions, every sacrifice, suddenly tasted like ash.

[story 2]

"This is it," Professor Albright had said, his voice booming with pride as he handed me the envelope. I’d practically vibrated with excitement, the relief washing over me like a warm tide. But when I opened it, the carefully planned trajectory of my future fractured. The name listed at the top belonged to someone else—a distant cousin, perhaps, or someone I’d never met. A sudden, sharp pain bloomed behind my eyes. I stood by the window, watching the autumn leaves swirl down, each descent feeling like a judgment. I clenched my fists until my knuckles turned white, the pressure a desperate attempt to hold myself together. I felt a profound sense of foolishness wash over me, a heavy cloak of self-reproach settling deep into my bones.

[story 3]

I kept rereading the confirmation email, the words blurring into meaningless shapes. The scholarship money, the opportunity—it all belonged to another soul. A tightness constricted my throat, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I walked to my desk, the wood feeling cold and unforgiving beneath my palms. I picked up my pen, intending to write a thank-you note, but the ink refused to flow. Instead, my gaze drifted to the framed photo on my shelf—a picture of my family smiling brightly. The smile felt alien now, disconnected from the hollow echo in my stomach. I leaned my forehead against the cool surface of the desk, letting out a slow, ragged exhale. The silence of the room amplified the sound of my own unsteady heartbeat, a frantic rhythm against the backdrop of utter disappointment.
